source_papers.title: Supplemented phase diagrams for the vitrification CPA cocktails DP6, VS55 and M22
Physical and biological aspects of renal vitrification
Cryopreservation of Complex Systems: The Missing Link in the Regenerative Medicine Supply Chain (Review)

extraction_metadata.extraction_notes: The three major vitrification cocktails (DP6, VS55, M22) are extremely well-documented across many papers. Composition data is high confidence. Thermal property values are from multiple corroborating sources. This represents the core 'gold standard' data for the knowledge base.

formulations.name: DP6

Tg.notes: Values vary slightly across papers: -115 to -122°C range reported

CCR.notes: Requires relatively fast cooling — limits applicability to small volumes

CWR.notes: Very high warming rate required — major practical limitation

biological_outcomes.outcome: Successful vitrification of small cartilage samples with good post-thaw viability

toxicity_profile.notes: Lower toxicity than VS55 or M22 due to lower total molarity and absence of formamide. Main limitation is high CCR/CWR requirement, not toxicity.

formulations.practical_notes: DP6 is the 'gentler' cocktail — less toxic but harder to vitrify (needs fast cooling/warming). Best suited for small samples where rapid thermal change is achievable. Often used with nanowarming to address high CWR.

formulations.name: VS55

CCR.notes: Much more achievable than DP6 — compatible with moderate-sized samples

CWR.notes: Still challenging for large organs but manageable for tissues

biological_outcomes.outcome: Successful vitrification and recovery of vascular grafts

toxicity_profile.notes: Formamide adds toxicity risk. Requires careful step-wise introduction and removal. Exposure time at higher temperatures is critical. The 3-component approach balances toxicity (no single agent at very high concentration) against vitrification tendency.

formulations.practical_notes: VS55 is the 'workhorse' cocktail — good balance of vitrification tendency and manageable toxicity. The inclusion of formamide is controversial (toxic) but dramatically lowers CCR/CWR requirements. Has the longest track record in tissue vitrification research.

formulations.name: M22

CCR.notes: Extraordinarily low — can vitrify at near-passive cooling rates. This is the key achievement of M22.

CWR.notes: Extremely low CWR — practical for large organs. Ice blockers (X-1000, Z-1000) are the key innovation.

biological_outcomes.outcome: First successful vitrification and transplantation of a whole mammalian kidney. Rabbit kidneys were vitrified with M22, rewarmed, transplanted, and supported life as sole kidney.
biological_outcomes.viability_metric: organ function post-transplant, life support as sole kidney

biological_outcomes.outcome: High viability recovery in cortical slices — K/Na ratio recovery >80% of control
Structural preservation and some electrophysiological recovery reported in recent studies (2025)
biological_outcomes.notes: Recent bioRxiv preprints — active area of research

toxicity_profile.notes: M22 achieves low CCR/CWR at the cost of high total solute concentration (9.3M). Toxicity is managed through: (1) step-wise CPA introduction at low temperature, (2) careful control of exposure time, (3) use of carrier solution with osmotic buffering. The ice blockers (X-1000, Z-1000) allow lower penetrating CPA concentrations than would otherwise be needed.

formulations.practical_notes: M22 is the most advanced vitrification solution for organ-scale preservation. Developed by Greg Fahy at 21st Century Medicine over decades. The key innovations are: (1) multi-component approach distributes toxicity, (2) ice blockers suppress ice nucleation without adding osmotic load, (3) the combination achieves CCR/CWR low enough for whole organs. The formulation is complex and somewhat proprietary (ice blockers are specialty chemicals).
